beginner.question: What is the difference between an absolute and a relative file path?
beginner.answer: An absolute path is a full URL (https://example.com/file.jpg). A relative path is specified relative to the current file's location (images/file.jpg).

beginner.question: What does '../' mean in a file path?
beginner.answer: '../' means 'go up one directory level'. Use '../../' to go up two levels, and so on.

beginner.question: How do you reference an image in a subfolder called 'images'?
beginner.answer: Use images/filename.jpg from the page referencing it. If the page is in a subfolder, you may need ../images/filename.jpg.

beginner.question: What is a root-relative path?
beginner.answer: A root-relative path starts with / and is relative to the website root: /images/logo.png. It works the same from any page.

beginner.question: Should you use forward slashes or backslashes in HTML paths?
beginner.answer: Always use forward slashes (/). Backslashes (\\) are Windows-specific and not valid in web URLs.

beginner.question: What happens if an image file path is incorrect?
beginner.answer: The browser shows a broken image icon (alt text may be displayed) and a 404 error in the network tab.

beginner.question: How do you link to a CSS file in a css/ folder?
beginner.answer: Use <link rel='stylesheet' href='css/style.css'> from a root-level HTML file.

beginner.question: What is the current directory represented by in path syntax?
beginner.answer: The current directory is represented by ./ (optional). ./file.jpg is the same as file.jpg.

beginner.question: How do filenames with spaces affect file paths?
beginner.answer: Spaces must be URL-encoded as %20, creating messy URLs. It's better to use hyphens: 'my-image.jpg'.

beginner.question: From about.html in the root, how do you link to index.html in the same folder?
beginner.answer: Simply use index.html or ./index.html since they are in the same directory.

intermediate.question: How are CSS file paths resolved differently from HTML file paths?
intermediate.answer: CSS paths (like url('./bg.png') in a stylesheet) are resolved relative to the CSS file's location, not the HTML page. This often causes confusion when CSS is in a subfolder.

intermediate.question: What is the difference between root-relative and relative paths for shared components?
intermediate.answer: Root-relative paths (/css/style.css) work from any page depth, making them ideal for headers, footers, and navigation. Relative paths (../../css/style.css) must be adjusted per page.

intermediate.question: How do server-side includes and templating engines handle file paths?
intermediate.answer: Server-side includes (SSI, PHP, etc.) resolve paths from the file system root or the server root, not the web root. Templating engines often use a 'base path' or 'template directory' as the reference point.

intermediate.question: What are canonical URLs and how do they affect file paths?
intermediate.answer: A canonical URL tells search engines which URL is the authoritative version. If relative paths are used with different canonical URLs, the same content might be indexed under different paths, causing duplicate content issues.

intermediate.question: How do Content Delivery Networks (CDNs) affect file path strategy?
intermediate.answer: CDNs require absolute paths or protocol-relative URLs (//cdn.example.com/file.js). When switching between development and production, base URLs often change, making relative paths impractical for CDN resources.

intermediate.question: How does the <base> element affect relative file paths?
intermediate.answer: The <base> element sets a base URL for all relative links in the document. With <base href='https://example.com/sub/'> , a link to 'page.html' resolves to https://example.com/sub/page.html instead of the current page's directory.

intermediate.question: What is a 'directory traversal' vulnerability and how does it relate to file paths?
intermediate.answer: Directory traversal is a security attack where user input containing '../' is used to access files outside the intended directory. Web applications must sanitize user-supplied file paths to prevent accessing system files.

intermediate.question: How do build tools (Webpack, Vite) transform file paths?
intermediate.answer: Build tools resolve import paths at build time, often converting relative source paths to hashed output paths (style.css → style.abc123.css). They also handle path resolution for assets, replacing development paths with production CDN paths.

intermediate.question: What is URL encoding and when is it needed in file paths?
intermediate.answer: URL encoding replaces unsafe characters with % followed by two hex digits. Spaces → %20, special characters like # → %23. Browsers often do this automatically, but it's best to avoid special characters entirely.

intermediate.question: How do symbolic links and aliases on servers affect file paths?
intermediate.answer: Symbolic links (symlinks) on Linux servers can create paths that don't match the physical file structure. A link at /images might point to /var/storage/images. This can confuse relative path calculations.

advanced.question: How does the browser resolve relative URLs in the context of the <base> element?
advanced.answer: When a <base> tag exists, the browser uses its href as the base URL for all relative URLs. Without <base>, the base URL is the current document's URL. Fragment-only URLs (#section) always refer to the current document, regardless of <base>.

advanced.question: What is the difference between URI, URL, and URN and how do they apply to file paths?
advanced.answer: URI is the general term for resource identification. URL (Uniform Resource Locator) is a type of URI that includes location information (https://example.com/file.jpg). URN (Uniform Resource Name) identifies by name (urn:isbn:1234). HTML uses URLs for file paths.

advanced.question: How do path resolution algorithms differ between browsers and Node.js?
advanced.answer: Browsers resolve paths relative to the current page URL or <base> href. Node.js resolves paths relative to the current module's __dirname or process.cwd(). The syntax (./, ../) is similar, but the reference points differ.

advanced.question: What is the 'path' component of a URL and how does URL parsing handle relative paths?
advanced.answer: The URL path component includes everything between the domain and the query string. Browsers parse paths by splitting on '/' and resolving '.' and '..' segments. Query strings (?key=val) and fragments (#hash) are not part of the path.

advanced.question: How do Service Workers and Cache Storage APIs interact with file paths?
advanced.answer: Service Worker URLs are resolved relative to the Service Worker script's location. Cached resources use the full URL as the cache key. Relative paths in cached pages may fail if the base URL doesn't match the cache key.

advanced.question: What is a 'data URL' and how does it serve as an alternative to file paths?
advanced.answer: A data URL embeds the resource content directly in the URL: data:image/png;base64,iVBOR.... This eliminates the need for a separate file and network request but increases HTML size and isn't cacheable.

advanced.question: How do Content Security Policy (CSP) path restrictions work?
advanced.answer: CSP's 'script-src' and other directives can use path restrictions: script-src https://example.com/js/ allows scripts only from that path. The path must be an absolute path or prefix match. Wildcards and relative paths are not supported in CSP sources.

advanced.question: How do relative paths behave differently in iframes?
advanced.answer: An iframe's content resolves relative URLs based on the iframe's src attribute (or srcdoc), not the parent page's URL. This means the same relative path can point to different resources depending on the iframe's context.

advanced.question: What is percent-encoding and how does it interact with file paths containing non-ASCII characters?
advanced.answer: Percent-encoding (URL encoding) converts non-ASCII and special characters to %hex format. Japanese characters (日本語), emoji (🚀), and accented letters (é) must be encoded. Browsers typically auto-encode, but some servers may reject non-encoded paths.

advanced.question: Explain the complete URL resolution algorithm as specified by the WHATWG URL Standard.
advanced.answer: The algorithm: (1) Parse the base URL. (2) If the input is an absolute URL (has scheme), use it directly. (3) Otherwise, resolve relative to the base URL's path by: removing the last segment of the base path if the relative path doesn't start with '/', then applying '..' and '.' segments, then appending the relative path. Finally, parse the result and return a complete URL record.
